enrichment.short_description: Data Miner is a Chrome and Edge browser extension that scrapes tables and structured data from web pages and exports the results to CSV, Excel, or Google Sheets. It uses point-and-click recipes, community-shared templates for popular sites, and custom JavaScript rules to extract data without writing code. It is built and maintained by Software Innovation Lab LLC of Seattle, Washington.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription, billed monthly by number of pages scraped
enrichment.starting_price: Free for up to 500 pages per month; paid plans start at 19.99 USD per month
enrichment.company_size: Small, privately held company (exact headcount not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: Data Miner Review 2026: Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Data Miner is a Chrome and Edge extension for scraping web tables and data into CSV or Excel. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Data Miner is a browser extension for Google Chrome and Microsoft Edge that lets users extract, or scrape, structured data from web pages and convert it into a CSV file, an Excel spreadsheet, or a Google Sheet. It is developed and maintained by Software Innovation Lab LLC, a small software company based in Seattle, Washington. Data Miner is aimed at non-technical users such as sales, marketing, research, and e-commerce professionals who need to pull data such as product listings, prices, reviews, contact information, or search results from websites without writing custom scraping code. The core mechanism behind Data Miner is the 'recipe', a saved set of extraction rules tied to a specific website or page pattern. Data Miner ships with a large public library of community-contributed and company-maintained recipes for popular sites including major e-commerce marketplaces, business directories, job boards, and social networks, so many users can click a button and immediately pull data from a supported site. For sites without a pre-built recipe, Data Miner provides a point-and-click recipe editor: users click on the data they want, such as a price, a name, or a table column, and Data Miner infers a reusable rule they can save, edit, and share. Power users can extend recipes with custom JavaScript to clean, transform, or filter scraped values before export, for example stripping currency symbols or parsing an email address out of a block of text. Beyond single-page scraping, Data Miner supports multi-page automation. It can follow next-page links, paginated search results, or infinite-scroll patterns to crawl through many pages in one run, and it can fill in web forms from a CSV of input values to automate repetitive lookups. It can also scrape data that sits behind a login or inside a private intranet, because it runs inside the user's own authenticated browser session rather than through an external crawler with no access to that session. Exports go to local CSV or Excel files, or directly into a connected Google Sheet, which keeps a running spreadsheet updated as new scrapes are run. Data Miner uses a freemium, page-count-based pricing model. The free plan allows up to 500 page scrapes per month with access to public recipes and basic automation, but restricts access to some domains and to private recipes. Paid plans, Solo, Small Business, Business, and Business Plus, unlock private recipe creation, unrestricted domain access, custom JavaScript, Google Sheets integration, and email support, and scale by monthly page-scrape limits from 500 pages up to 9,000 pages, priced from about 19.99 USD to 200 USD per month. Larger organizations that need higher volumes, server-side scraping instead of local browser scraping, or long-term contracts can request custom Enterprise pricing. Because Data Miner runs entirely inside the browser and relies on the user's own IP address and session, it does not include built-in proxy rotation, a hosted scraping API, or scheduled cloud-based crawls, functionality found in dedicated scraping platforms. This makes it well suited to ad hoc, occasional, or moderate-volume data pulls by an individual user, but less suited to large-scale, automated, unattended scraping pipelines. Typical use cases include competitive price monitoring, lead list building from directories, real estate or job listing aggregation, and pulling structured tables out of government or research websites for analysis. Reviewers generally rate it highly for ease of use and accuracy on supported sites, while noting the lack of proxies, API access, and scale as the main limitations for heavier scraping workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Data Miner Works
paragraphs: Data Miner runs as a browser extension inside Chrome or Edge, so it scrapes pages using the same session and cookies as the user who is browsing, which lets it reach pages behind a login or inside a private network that an external, headless scraper could never access.

Extraction rules are stored as reusable recipes. Users can start from a public recipe built for a specific website, or build a new one by clicking directly on the data they want, and Data Miner will infer a rule that can be saved, edited, or shared with a team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Data Miner Pricing and Plans
paragraphs: Data Miner uses a page-count-based freemium model. The Free plan covers up to 500 page scrapes a month with access to public recipes, while paid Solo, Small Business, Business, and Business Plus plans raise the monthly page limit from 500 up to 9,000 pages and add private recipes, custom JavaScript, and Google Sheets export.
Organizations that need higher volumes, server-based scraping instead of local browser scraping, or a long-term contract can request custom Enterprise pricing directly from Data Miner's team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Limitations to Know Before Choosing Data Miner
paragraphs: Because Data Miner scrapes through the user's own browser and IP address, it has no built-in proxy rotation, no hosted scraping API, and no way to run unattended in the background without the browser open, which sets it apart from infrastructure-style scraping platforms aimed at developers.
It is best suited to occasional or moderate-volume scraping by an individual user rather than large-scale, automated data pipelines that need to run continuously without human interaction.

features.title: Point-and-click recipe builder
features.description: Click directly on the data you want on a page and Data Miner infers a reusable extraction rule without any coding.

features.title: Public recipe library
features.description: A large library of community and company-maintained recipes lets users scrape many popular sites with one click.

features.title: Multi-page automation
features.description: Follows next-page links, paginated results, and infinite scroll to crawl and extract data across many pages in one run.

features.title: Custom JavaScript rules
features.description: Power users can write custom JavaScript to clean, transform, or filter scraped values before export.

features.title: Form-fill automation
features.description: Fills in web forms from a CSV of input values to automate repetitive lookups across many records.

features.title: Google Sheets export
features.description: Sends scraped data directly into a connected Google Sheet in addition to local CSV or Excel files.

features.title: Login and firewall access
features.description: Scrapes pages behind a login or inside a private network using the user's own authenticated browser session.

features.title: One-click table extraction
features.description: Right-click any table on a page to extract it directly without building a custom recipe.

pros: Easy to learn for non-developers
Large library of ready-made recipes for popular sites
Works on password-protected and internal pages
Multiple export formats including Google Sheets
Free tier available for light, occasional usage
cons: No built-in proxy rotation or IP masking
No hosted scraping API for developers
Monthly page-scrape limits can be restrictive at scale
Requires a running browser session rather than a background service
Anti-scraping defenses on some sites can break recipes over time

faqs.question: What is Data Miner used for?
faqs.answer: Data Miner is used to scrape tables and structured data from web pages and export the results to CSV, Excel, or Google Sheets, commonly for price monitoring, lead lists, and research.

faqs.question: Is Data Miner free?
faqs.answer: Data Miner has a free plan covering up to 500 page scrapes per month, with paid plans starting at 19.99 USD per month for additional volume and features.

faqs.question: Does Data Miner work in Microsoft Edge?
faqs.answer: Yes, Data Miner is available as an extension for both Google Chrome and Microsoft Edge.

faqs.question: Can Data Miner scrape data behind a login?
faqs.answer: Yes, because it runs inside the user's own browser session, it can scrape pages that require a login or that sit on a private intranet.

faqs.question: What file formats does Data Miner export to?
faqs.answer: Data Miner exports to CSV files, Excel spreadsheets, and directly into connected Google Sheets.

faqs.question: Does Data Miner have an API?
faqs.answer: Data Miner does not offer a hosted scraping API; it operates as a browser extension rather than a developer API service.

faqs.question: How is Data Miner priced?
faqs.answer: Data Miner is priced by monthly page-scrape volume across Free, Solo, Small Business, Business, and Business Plus tiers, with custom Enterprise pricing available.

faqs.question: Is Data Miner safe to use?
faqs.answer: Data Miner states it does not sell or share user data, and it operates within the user's own browser rather than routing data through third-party servers by default.

use_cases.title: Competitive price monitoring
use_cases.description: Track competitor product prices and listings on e-commerce sites by scraping product pages into a spreadsheet.

use_cases.title: Lead list building
use_cases.description: Pull business names, contact details, and other information from directories into a CSV for outreach.

use_cases.title: Real estate and job listing aggregation
use_cases.description: Collect listings from multiple pages of a real estate or job site into one spreadsheet for analysis.

use_cases.title: Public data research
use_cases.description: Extract tables from government, academic, or research websites for further analysis in Excel.

enrichment.short_description: Data Rescue is a data recovery application for Mac and Windows made by Prosoft Engineering, used to recover deleted, formatted, or corrupted files from hard drives, SSDs, and removable media. It scans a drive, lets users preview recoverable files before paying, and then restores them to a separate destination. Prosoft Engineering has published the Data Rescue product line since 2002.
enrichment.pricing_model: One-time and annual license options, plus a free scan-and-preview trial
enrichment.starting_price: Free trial recovers up to 1GB; paid Standard license starts at 79 USD for 30 days of unlimited recovery
enrichment.company_size: Small, privately held company (exact headcount not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: Data Rescue Review 2026: Pricing, Features, Pros and Cons
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Data Rescue by Prosoft Engineering recovers deleted files on Mac and Windows. See 2026 pricing plans, key features, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Data Rescue is a data recovery software application published by Prosoft Engineering, Inc., a company founded in 1985 and based in Pleasanton, California. Prosoft has sold versions of Data Rescue since 2002, and the product has gone through multiple major versions, with Data Rescue 6 being the current generation available for both macOS and Windows from a single license. Data Rescue is designed for consumers, IT professionals, and small businesses who need to recover files that were accidentally deleted, lost when a drive was formatted or reformatted, or made inaccessible because a drive will not mount, will not boot, or has developed file system corruption. The recovery workflow follows three steps: scan, preview, and recover. Users choose between a Quick Scan, intended for drives that will not boot or mount and that searches for a known, previously cataloged file system structure, and a Deep Scan, a more thorough sector-by-sector search used for drives that have been reformatted or that have more extensive data loss. Data Rescue can recover files from internal hard disk drives and solid-state drives, external drives, USB flash drives, SD and Compact Flash cards, and other removable media, and it supports the NTFS, APFS, HFS+, FAT, and exFAT file systems used by Windows and Mac computers. A feature called FileIQ lets the software learn to recognize new or unlisted file signatures during a scan, which helps it identify file types that are not in its standard built-in list. Before paying for a license, users can preview the names and, for many file types, the contents of recoverable files, so they can judge whether a paid recovery is worthwhile. Data Rescue's Advanced Mode adds tools aimed at more technical users and IT professionals, including a hex viewer for inspecting raw disk data, a secure erase function, the ability to save and manage as many as 15 separate scan sessions, support for RAID 0 and JBOD arrays, a virtual RAID simulation feature for rebuilding arrays before committing to a recovery, and low-level allocation block layout analysis. On the Mac, Data Rescue can also build a bootable recovery drive, which allows a technician to boot a Mac from external media when its internal startup drive has failed, and scan or recover files without ever writing to the failing drive itself. The software recognizes more than 100 distinct file types out of the box, spanning documents such as PDF, Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and QuickBooks files, images including TIFF, PNG, RAW camera formats, and Adobe Illustrator files, and audio formats such as MP3, WAV, AAC, and Logic Pro session files. Pricing follows a tiered model. Every user can run a free trial that scans and previews an entire drive and recovers up to 1GB of data at no cost, which is enough to test whether important files are recoverable before paying. Beyond the trial, a Standard license, priced at 79 USD, unlocks unlimited data recovery for 30 days, making it suited to a single, one-time recovery job. For users or professionals who expect to need data recovery repeatedly, a Professional license, priced at roughly 399.96 USD per year, provides unlimited recoveries across drives and devices for the length of the subscription, and one license can be used across both the Mac and Windows builds of the software. Data Rescue requires a working secondary storage device to hold recovered files, since recovered data should never be written back to the same drive being scanned, and the current Mac build supports Apple Silicon Macs as well as a documented range of macOS versions. Prosoft Engineering also sells Drive Genius, a separate Mac disk maintenance and optimization utility, and offers paid, hands-on data recovery services for customers with especially difficult cases such as physically damaged drives or failed RAID arrays that self-service software cannot resolve.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Data Rescue Recovery Process
paragraphs: Data Rescue follows a three-step workflow: scan the affected drive using either Quick Scan or Deep Scan, preview the files that were found before paying anything, and then recover the chosen files to a separate, healthy destination drive.

The FileIQ feature lets the software learn and recognize file types it does not already have in its built-in list, which can improve recovery results on drives containing unusual or specialized file formats.

long_form_blocks.heading: Data Rescue Pricing and Licensing
paragraphs: New users can run a free trial that scans and previews an entire drive and recovers up to 1GB of data at no charge, which is enough to confirm whether important files are recoverable before committing to a paid license.

Beyond the trial, a Standard license priced around 79 USD unlocks unlimited recovery for 30 days, aimed at a single recovery job, while a Professional license priced at roughly 399.96 USD per year suits users or technicians who expect to need recovery repeatedly across the year.

long_form_blocks.heading: Advanced Mode for IT Professionals
paragraphs: Advanced Mode adds a hex viewer, secure erase, support for up to 15 saved scan sessions, RAID 0 and JBOD support with virtual RAID simulation, and low-level allocation block layout analysis, aimed at technicians handling more complex recovery cases.
On Mac, Data Rescue can also create a bootable recovery drive, letting a technician boot from external media and scan or recover a failed startup drive without writing any new data to it.

features.title: Quick Scan and Deep Scan modes
features.description: Choose a fast scan for unmountable or non-booting drives or a thorough sector-by-sector scan for reformatted drives.

features.title: FileIQ adaptive file recognition
features.description: Learns to recognize new or unlisted file signatures during a scan to improve recovery of unusual file types.

features.title: Pre-recovery file preview
features.description: Preview recoverable file names and, for many formats, contents before paying for a license.

features.title: Broad file system support
features.description: Supports NTFS, APFS, HFS+, FAT, and exFAT file systems across Mac and Windows drives.

features.title: RAID and JBOD recovery
features.description: Supports RAID 0 and JBOD arrays with a virtual RAID simulation feature for rebuilding arrays before recovery.

features.title: Bootable recovery drive
features.description: On Mac, creates a bootable external drive to recover a failed startup disk without writing to it.

features.title: Advanced Mode tools
features.description: Includes a hex viewer, secure erase, and allocation block layout analysis for technical users.

features.title: Wide media support
features.description: Recovers from internal and external hard drives, SSDs, USB drives, and SD and Compact Flash cards.

pros: Free trial lets users confirm recoverability before paying
Works across both Mac and Windows from one license
Supports a wide range of file systems and over 100 file types
Advanced Mode tools suit IT professionals and technicians
Long-standing company with decades of data recovery experience
cons: Deep Scan can take many hours on large or badly damaged drives
Professional annual license is a recurring cost for occasional users
Advanced Mode features can overwhelm non-technical users
Cannot guarantee recovery on physically damaged drives
Requires a separate destination drive with enough free space

faqs.question: What is Data Rescue used for?
faqs.answer: Data Rescue is used to recover deleted, lost, or inaccessible files from hard drives, SSDs, and removable media on Mac and Windows computers.

faqs.question: Does Data Rescue work on both Mac and Windows?
faqs.answer: Yes, a single Data Rescue license can be used to run the software on both macOS and Windows.

faqs.question: How much data can I recover for free?
faqs.answer: The free trial allows scanning and previewing a whole drive and recovering up to 1GB of data at no cost.

faqs.question: Can Data Rescue recover files from a formatted drive?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Deep Scan mode is designed to search reformatted or heavily damaged drives for recoverable files.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Quick Scan and Deep Scan?
faqs.answer: Quick Scan is faster and targets drives that will not boot or mount, while Deep Scan performs a thorough sector-by-sector search for reformatted or more extensively damaged drives.

faqs.question: Does Data Rescue support RAID recovery?
faqs.answer: Yes, Advanced Mode supports RAID 0 and JBOD arrays and includes a virtual RAID simulation tool.

faqs.question: How long does a Data Rescue scan take?
faqs.answer: Scan time depends on drive size and condition; a Deep Scan on a large or badly damaged drive can take several hours.

faqs.question: Who makes Data Rescue?
faqs.answer: Data Rescue is made by Prosoft Engineering, Inc., a company founded in 1985 and based in Pleasanton, California.

use_cases.title: Recovering accidentally deleted files
use_cases.description: Restore personal documents, photos, or other files that were deleted or removed from the trash.

use_cases.title: Restoring a non-booting startup drive
use_cases.description: Scan and recover data from a Mac or Windows drive that will not mount or boot normally.

use_cases.title: Recovering media from cameras and SD cards
use_cases.description: Retrieve photos and video lost from SD cards, Compact Flash cards, and other camera media.

use_cases.title: Rebuilding data from failed RAID arrays
use_cases.description: Use RAID 0 and JBOD support with virtual RAID simulation to recover data from a failed array.

enrichment.short_description: Dataimporter.io is a cloud-based data loading tool that imports, cleans, and migrates CSV and Excel data into Salesforce. It supports scheduled imports, deduplication, rollback, and connections to external systems such as Dropbox and SFTP, and offers a free tier alongside paid, per-user monthly plans. The company was founded in 2021 and is based in Auckland, New Zealand.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium, per-user monthly subscription with usage-based record limits
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan for up to 20,000 records; paid plans start at 109 USD per user per month billed annually
enrichment.company_size: Small, privately held company (exact headcount not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dataimporter.io imports CSV and Excel data into Salesforce with cleaning, deduplication, and scheduling. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Dataimporter.io, commonly known as Dataimporter, is a cloud-based data loading and data migration tool purpose-built for Salesforce. Founded in 2021 and based in Auckland, New Zealand, the company positions its product as an easier, more forgiving alternative to Salesforce's native Data Import Wizard and to older desktop tools, aiming to let Salesforce administrators, consultants, and revenue operations teams load, clean, transform, and migrate data without deep technical expertise or command-line tooling. At its core, Dataimporter lets users upload CSV or Excel files, or connect to external systems such as Dropbox, SFTP servers, and Heroku, and map source columns to Salesforce fields through a guided interface. It supports multi-object imports in a single job, so related parent and child records, such as Accounts and Contacts, or Opportunities and their line items, can be loaded together with relationships preserved, rather than as separate, manually sequenced steps. Before committing a full load, users can run the import against a sample of records to catch mapping or formatting errors early, reducing the risk of a bad load reaching production data. If a load does go wrong, Dataimporter can roll back an entire import job, undoing the records it created or changed, which is a safety feature not available in many lower-level data loading tools. Beyond one-off manual imports, Dataimporter supports scheduled, recurring import tasks, so a business can automatically sync a nightly export file from another system into Salesforce without a person re-running the job by hand. It includes record lookup by text value, matching related records by a human-readable field instead of only by Salesforce ID, deduplication logic to prevent duplicate records from being created on repeated loads, configurable date formats and API settings for organizations with specific data standards, and a data cleaning feature on paid plans that can normalize or fix values during the import process. For organizations managing multiple Salesforce orgs, Dataimporter offers a dedicated Salesforce-to-Salesforce connector used to migrate data between orgs, seed sandboxes with realistic data for testing, deploy configuration, and merge orgs during company reorganizations or Salesforce consolidations, use cases that typically require custom scripting with other tools. Dataimporter follows a freemium, per-user, monthly subscription pricing model with tiers gated by monthly record volume, backup storage, and number of production Salesforce instances supported. The Free plan supports up to 20,000 records, 1GB of backup storage, one production instance, one integration, and community support, making it usable for small nonprofits, startups, or light administrative use. The Premium plan, priced at 109 USD per user per month when billed annually, or 129 USD billed monthly, raises the limit to 150,000 records per month, 10GB of backup storage, two production instances, the data cleaning feature, and email support. The Enterprise plan, at 299 USD per user per month billed annually, or 349 USD monthly, extends this further to 1 million records per month, 50GB of backup storage, five production instances, migration templates, and priority support. Organizations with larger or more specialized needs, such as single sign-on, advanced security controls, customer-managed storage, or bring-your-own-key encryption, can move to a Custom plan with negotiated, unlimited usage and backup. All plans include core import capabilities such as rollback, retry, multi-object import, formula fields, filters, and deduplication, while the higher tiers add data governance features like data residency control and downloadable import history.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Dataimporter.io Imports Data into Salesforce
paragraphs: Users upload a CSV or Excel file, or connect a source such as Dropbox, SFTP, or Heroku, then map source columns to Salesforce fields through a guided interface that supports multiple related objects in a single job, such as Accounts and their child Contacts.

Before running a full import, users can test the mapping against a sample of records, and if a completed import turns out to contain errors, the entire job can be rolled back, undoing the records it created or changed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Dataimporter.io Pricing Tiers
paragraphs: The Free plan supports up to 20,000 records, 1GB of backup storage, and one production Salesforce instance. The Premium plan, at 109 USD per user per month billed annually, raises this to 150,000 records a month and adds data cleaning and email support.

The Enterprise plan, at 299 USD per user per month billed annually, supports up to 1 million records a month, five production instances, migration templates, and priority support, while a Custom plan adds single sign-on, advanced security, and unlimited usage for larger organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Org Migration and Ongoing Automation
paragraphs: A dedicated Salesforce-to-Salesforce connector is used to migrate data between orgs, seed sandboxes with realistic test data, and merge orgs during company reorganizations, use cases that would otherwise require custom scripting.

Scheduled, recurring import tasks let a business automatically sync a regularly generated export file from another system into Salesforce, removing the need to manually rerun the same import each time.

Rollback reduces risk of bad data loads
No coding or command-line skills required
Supports scheduled, automated recurring imports
Dedicated org-to-org migration and sandbox seeding tools
cons: Per-user pricing can add up for larger teams
Higher record volumes require Premium or Enterprise tiers
Enterprise-grade security features are reserved for higher-priced plans
Limited to Salesforce as a destination, unlike general-purpose ETL tools
Complex data cleaning transformations may still require manual review

faqs.question: What is Dataimporter.io used for?
faqs.answer: Dataimporter.io is used to import, clean, and migrate CSV and Excel data into Salesforce, and to move data between Salesforce orgs.

faqs.question: Does Dataimporter.io have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Free plan supports up to 20,000 records, 1GB of backup storage, and one production Salesforce instance.

faqs.question: Can Dataimporter.io migrate data between two Salesforce orgs?
faqs.answer: Yes, a dedicated Salesforce-to-Salesforce connector supports org-to-org migration, sandbox seeding, and org merges.

faqs.question: Does Dataimporter.io support scheduled imports?
faqs.answer: Yes, recurring import tasks can be scheduled to automatically sync data on a regular basis.

faqs.question: What file formats does Dataimporter.io accept?
faqs.answer: Dataimporter.io accepts CSV and Excel files for manual upload, in addition to connecting to sources like Dropbox and SFTP.

faqs.question: Can a bad import be undone in Dataimporter.io?
faqs.answer: Yes, the rollback feature can undo an entire import job, reversing the records it created or changed.

faqs.question: How is Dataimporter.io priced?
faqs.answer: Dataimporter.io is priced per user per month across Free, Premium, Enterprise, and Custom tiers, gated by monthly record volume and features.

faqs.question: Where is Dataimporter.io based?
faqs.answer: Dataimporter.io is based in Auckland, New Zealand, and was founded in 2021.

enrichment.short_description: Datalore is a cloud-based, Jupyter-compatible data science notebook environment made by JetBrains, supporting Python, R, Kotlin, and SQL with real-time collaborative editing, AI-assisted coding, and one-click interactive reports. It is available as a managed cloud service or as a self-hosted On-Premises deployment for teams with strict data governance needs. JetBrains first released Datalore in November 2018.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription with a free Cloud tier, a paid Cloud team tier, and custom On-Premises licensing
enrichment.starting_price: Free Cloud plan available; paid Cloud plans and On-Premises pricing available on request
enrichment.company_size: JetBrains employs over 1000 people worldwide (Datalore team size not separately disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: Datalore by JetBrains Review 2026: Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Datalore is JetBrains' collaborative, Jupyter-compatible data science notebook. See 2026 pricing, AI coding features, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Datalore is a cloud-based data science notebook platform built by JetBrains, the software company best known for developer tools such as IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, and Kotlin. JetBrains first launched Datalore in November 2018 as a web-based environment for data analysis and machine learning in Python, and has since expanded it into a broader, Jupyter-compatible collaborative workspace that also supports R, Kotlin, and SQL. JetBrains is headquartered in Prague, Czech Republic, and Datalore is offered both as a JetBrains-managed cloud product and as a self-hosted On-Premises deployment for organizations that need to keep notebooks and data inside their own infrastructure. Datalore's core proposition is real-time collaboration on notebooks that remain compatible with the standard Jupyter format, so teams can co-edit a notebook simultaneously the way they would co-edit a shared document, see each other's cursors and changes live, and share notebooks by link or email invitation with view-only or edit-level access. Each notebook runs in its own isolated compute environment with an integrated environment and package manager, built-in version history, and integrated cloud storage, removing the need for a data scientist to separately manage virtual environments, requirements files, or storage buckets. Datalore AI, the platform's AI coding assistant, helps users write Python, SQL, and R code faster, generate explanatory text around findings, and automatically fix code errors, aiming to speed up both exploratory analysis and the write-up of results. A one-click interactive report feature turns an ordinary analysis notebook into a shareable, presentation-style document that hides code cells from the audience while keeping charts, tables, and narrative text interactive, aimed at analysts who need to hand off findings to non-technical stakeholders without exporting to a separate tool such as a slide deck. Datalore is priced on a freemium basis. The Cloud Free plan is aimed at individual users and students, and includes unlimited notebooks with real-time collaboration, coding assistance, the ability to run two notebooks in parallel, one shared workspace, one interactive report, notebook export, and API access, backed by a resource allowance of 120 CPU-small machine hours and 10GB of cloud storage. The paid Cloud plan, aimed at teams and advanced analysts, adds Datalore AI, unlimited parallel notebooks, a reactive execution mode that automatically re-runs dependent cells when inputs change, full version history, unlimited database connections, unlimited reports and workspaces, scheduled notebook runs, and team management tools, with a resource allowance of 750 CPU-large machine hours per team member per month, access to GPU-backed machines, and 20GB of storage plus additional support; exact monthly or annual pricing for the Cloud plan is provided on request rather than published as a flat rate. A 14-day free trial of the Cloud product is available with a cap of three users and limited compute. Organizations that need to keep code and data entirely within their own network can license Datalore On-Premises, a self-hosted version with unlimited core features, internal usage plans configurable by an administrator, and full control over compute resources; On-Premises pricing is quote-based. Because Datalore is Jupyter-compatible, teams already using Jupyter notebooks, JupyterLab, or Google Colab can generally import and export their existing notebook files with minimal friction. Typical users include data science teams that want managed, governed collaboration without running their own JupyterHub infrastructure, analytics teams producing recurring reports for business stakeholders, and organizations in regulated industries that need the on-premises option to keep data science workloads inside their own security perimeter.

long_form_blocks.heading: Collaboration and AI Features in Datalore
paragraphs: Datalore notebooks support real-time, multi-user editing similar to a shared document, with notebooks shared by link or email invitation and either view or edit level access, and every notebook runs in its own isolated, managed environment.

Datalore AI assists with writing Python, SQL, and R code, generating explanatory text around findings, and fixing code errors, while the one-click interactive report feature turns a working notebook into a shareable, presentation-style document that hides code from the audience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cloud Free Plan versus Paid and On-Premises Options
paragraphs: The Cloud Free plan includes unlimited notebooks with collaboration, two parallel notebooks, one workspace, one interactive report, and 120 CPU-small machine hours with 10GB of storage, making it usable for individuals and students at no cost.

The paid Cloud plan adds Datalore AI, unlimited parallel notebooks, scheduled runs, and team management with a larger compute allowance, and pricing is provided on request; organizations needing to keep data on their own infrastructure can license the self-hosted On-Premises version instead.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Datalore Fits Best
paragraphs: Because Datalore is Jupyter-compatible, teams already working in Jupyter, JupyterLab, or Google Colab can generally import and export existing notebooks with minimal friction when adopting the platform.

It fits data science teams that want managed, governed collaboration without operating their own JupyterHub infrastructure, and organizations in regulated industries that need the On-Premises option to keep workloads inside their own security perimeter.

faqs.answer: Datalore is made by JetBrains, the company behind developer tools such as IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, and Kotlin.

enrichment.short_description: Datarails is a financial planning and analysis platform that lets finance teams keep working inside Excel while consolidating data from accounting, ERP, CRM, banking, and HR systems into a centralized, governed model. It adds automated reporting, budgeting, forecasting, scenario planning, and a generative AI assistant on top of familiar spreadsheets. Datarails was founded in 2015 and is headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel, with a United States base in New York.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom quote-based subscription, priced by user count, integrations, and company complexity
enrichment.starting_price: Pricing available on request; no published flat starting price
enrichment.headquarters: Tel Aviv, Israel (with a United States base in New York)
enrichment.seo_title: Datarails Review 2026: FP and A Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Datarails is an Excel-native FP and A platform for budgeting, forecasting, and reporting. See 2026 pricing structure, features, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Datarails is a financial planning and analysis, or FP and A, platform built around the idea that finance teams should not have to abandon Excel to get modern planning, reporting, and forecasting capabilities. Founded in 2015 by Didi Gurfinkel, Eyal Cohen, and Oded Har-Tal, the company is headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel, with an additional base in New York, and employs roughly 300 to 400 people across five continents. Datarails targets mid-market finance teams, particularly CFOs, financial planning and analysis managers, and controllers, who rely heavily on Excel for budgeting and reporting but struggle with manually consolidating data from many disconnected source systems. The platform's central component is Datarails Flex, an Excel add-in that lets analysts continue to build and edit models using native spreadsheet formulas, formatting, and workflows they already know, while the underlying data is pulled from a governed, cloud-hosted data layer instead of scattered local files. Datarails advertises more than 600 pre-built integrations spanning accounting systems, ERPs, CRMs, banking platforms, and HRIS software, which the platform uses to automatically consolidate financial and operational data into a single source of truth, removing much of the manual copy-and-paste and version-control chaos that comes from emailing spreadsheets between departments. A companion web application stores this consolidated data securely in the cloud, provides dashboards and centralized reporting, and gives finance leaders visibility across the whole organization rather than only within one analyst's local files. On top of consolidation, Datarails provides core FP and A functionality including budgeting and forecasting workflows, scenario and what-if planning, drill-down analysis that lets a user trace a summary figure back to the individual underlying transactions, version control across planning cycles, and automated refreshing of reports as new data arrives, which reduces the end-of-month scramble to manually update board decks and management reports. Datarails has also introduced a generative AI assistant, described by the company as one of the first of its kind purpose-built for FP and A, which can generate tailored summaries, variance explanations, and presentation-ready commentary from the underlying financial data, aiming to reduce the time analysts spend writing narrative commentary by hand. Datarails does not publish flat, self-serve pricing. Instead, the company structures its offering around a small number of tiers that progressively unlock more capability, starting from a reporting-focused entry tier and extending up to full operational finance automation with the broadest set of integrations and AI features, and pricing within those tiers scales with the number of users, the number and complexity of source-system integrations required, and the overall complexity of the organization's finance function, rather than a simple flat per-seat fee. Prospective customers request a demo and receive a tailored quote; support is included in the subscription at no additional charge. Because Datarails connects to many source systems and often requires building or adapting a company's existing Excel-based financial models to work inside the platform, most implementations involve a professional services engagement, commonly estimated in the range of 10,000 to 50,000 US dollars or more depending on the number of integrations, the complexity of the model being migrated, and the amount of user training and change management required. This implementation cost is separate from the ongoing subscription fee and is an important budgeting consideration for finance teams evaluating Datarails against lower-cost, more self-serve competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Datarails Keeps Finance Teams Working in Excel
paragraphs: Datarails Flex is an Excel add-in that lets analysts keep using native spreadsheet formulas and formatting while the underlying data comes from a governed, cloud-hosted layer instead of scattered local files emailed between team members.

More than 600 pre-built integrations across accounting, ERP, CRM, banking, and HR systems feed data automatically into this layer, reducing the manual copy-and-paste work and version-control confusion common in spreadsheet-based finance teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Planning, Reporting, and AI Features
paragraphs: The platform supports budgeting, forecasting, scenario and what-if planning, drill-down analysis down to individual transactions, version control across planning cycles, and automated report refreshes as new data arrives.

A generative AI assistant can produce tailored summaries, variance explanations, and presentation-ready commentary from the underlying financial data, aimed at reducing the time analysts spend writing narrative commentary by hand.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Structure and Implementation Costs
paragraphs: Datarails does not publish flat pricing. Instead it structures its offering around a small number of tiers that scale with user count, the number of integrations required, and overall organizational complexity, with pricing provided through a demo request.

Because Datarails connects to many source systems and often adapts a company's existing Excel models, most implementations involve a professional services engagement commonly estimated in the range of 10,000 to 50,000 US dollars or more, in addition to the ongoing subscription.

faqs.answer: Datarails is a financial planning and analysis platform that consolidates data from business systems into Excel-based budgeting, forecasting, and reporting workflows.

faqs.question: Is Datarails an Excel add-in or a standalone application?
faqs.answer: Datarails combines an Excel add-in called Datarails Flex with a companion cloud web application for centralized data storage and reporting.

faqs.question: How much does Datarails cost?
faqs.answer: Datarails pricing is not published; it is quoted based on user count, required integrations, and organizational complexity after a demo.

faqs.answer: Datarails advertises more than 600 integrations across accounting, ERP, CRM, banking, and HR systems.

faqs.answer: Datarails was founded in 2015 by Didi Gurfinkel, Eyal Cohen, and Oded Har-Tal.

faqs.answer: Most implementations involve a separate professional services engagement, commonly estimated at 10,000 to 50,000 US dollars or more.

faqs.answer: Datarails is built for mid-market finance teams, including CFOs, FP and A managers, and controllers who rely heavily on Excel.

enrichment.short_description: DataStax is a data platform company built on Apache Cassandra, best known for Astra DB, a cloud-native NoSQL and vector database used to power real-time and AI applications, and for Langflow, an open-source tool for building AI applications with large language models. Founded in 2010 as Riptano by Jonathan Ellis and Matt Pfeil, the company was later renamed DataStax and headquartered in Santa Clara, California. IBM announced its acquisition of DataStax in February 2025 and completed the deal in May 2025, folding DataStax's technology into IBM's watsonx AI and data portfolio.
enrichment.pricing_model: Usage-based cloud pricing for Astra DB plus enterprise licensing, now offered through IBM
enrichment.starting_price: Astra DB Free tier available with about 25 USD of monthly usage credit; Pay As You Go and Enterprise pricing scale from there
enrichment.company_size: Roughly 700 to 800 employees at the time of the IBM acquisition; now part of IBM
enrichment.headquarters: Santa Clara, California, United States (now operating as part of IBM)
enrichment.seo_title: DataStax Review 2026: Astra DB Pricing After IBM Deal
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DataStax builds Astra DB and Langflow on Apache Cassandra. See 2026 pricing, features, and what the 2025 IBM acquisition means for customers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: DataStax is a data infrastructure company built around Apache Cassandra, the open-source, highly scalable NoSQL database originally developed at Facebook and later donated to the Apache Software Foundation. The company was founded in 2010 in Austin, Texas, as Riptano, by Jonathan Ellis and Matt Pfeil, two engineers who had worked with Cassandra at Rackspace; it was soon renamed DataStax and its headquarters moved to Santa Clara, California, where the company remained based through the mid-2020s. Over more than a decade, DataStax built a commercial business around Cassandra, offering DataStax Enterprise, a supported, hardened distribution of Cassandra for large organizations, and later Astra DB, a fully managed, cloud-native database-as-a-service built on Cassandra that DataStax launched in May 2020. Astra DB removed much of the operational burden of running Cassandra clusters directly, letting developers provision a serverless database, pay based on usage, and scale storage and throughput without managing nodes themselves. As interest in generative AI and retrieval-augmented generation applications grew, DataStax positioned Astra DB as a vector database, adding native vector search and storage so the same database could hold structured application data alongside embeddings used to power AI features such as semantic search and chatbot memory. Alongside Astra DB, DataStax also develops and maintains Langflow, an open-source, low-code visual tool for building applications powered by large language models, which lets developers assemble AI application logic, including prompts, retrieval steps, agents, and tool calls, as a connected flow rather than hand-written orchestration code. DataStax also contributes to and supports several other open-source projects in this space, including Apache Pulsar and OpenSearch. On February 25, 2025, IBM announced it had agreed to acquire DataStax, aiming to strengthen its watsonx portfolio of AI and data products with DataStax's NoSQL, vector database, and generative AI tooling, and to give enterprise customers a stronger foundation for building and deploying AI applications on top of large volumes of unstructured and real-time data. The deal closed on May 28, 2025, and DataStax's technology, including Astra DB and Langflow, is now offered as part of IBM's product portfolio. IBM has stated it will continue to support and engage with the open-source communities DataStax was involved in, including Apache Cassandra, Langflow, Apache Pulsar, and OpenSearch, rather than closing those projects off. At the time of the acquisition, DataStax had roughly 700 to 800 employees, though as part of IBM its resources, sales channels, and enterprise reach have expanded considerably. Astra DB's pricing follows a usage-based, tiered structure. A Free tier gives developers a way to explore the product at no cost, providing a recurring monthly usage credit of around 25 USD that covers a meaningful amount of storage and read and write operations, commonly cited as up to about 80GB of storage and 20 million read and write operations per month, making it practical for prototypes, side projects, and small production workloads. Beyond the free tier, a Pay As You Go plan charges per operation and per gigabyte of storage or data transfer with no upfront commitment, and pricing varies somewhat by cloud provider, including AWS, Google Cloud, or Azure, and region. An Enterprise, or Standard, tier adds features aimed at larger organizations such as private network endpoints, customer-managed encryption keys, multi-region deployment, and centralized organization management, typically negotiated directly with sales rather than published as a flat rate. Existing DataStax Enterprise customers running self-managed Cassandra deployments can continue to license that product separately from the cloud-hosted Astra DB service.

long_form_blocks.heading: From Riptano to Astra DB
paragraphs: DataStax was founded in 2010 in Austin, Texas as Riptano by Jonathan Ellis and Matt Pfeil, two former Rackspace engineers who had worked with Apache Cassandra, before the company was renamed DataStax and its headquarters moved to Santa Clara, California.

DataStax launched Astra DB in May 2020 as a fully managed, serverless, cloud-native database-as-a-service built on Cassandra, removing much of the operational work of running Cassandra clusters directly, and later added native vector search so the same database could support AI applications alongside traditional workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: The 2025 IBM Acquisition
paragraphs: IBM announced its agreement to acquire DataStax on February 25, 2025, aiming to strengthen its watsonx portfolio with DataStax's NoSQL, vector database, and generative AI tooling, and the deal closed on May 28, 2025.

IBM has stated it will continue supporting the open-source communities DataStax was involved in, including Apache Cassandra, Langflow, Apache Pulsar, and OpenSearch, and DataStax's products, including Astra DB and Langflow, are now offered as part of IBM's product portfolio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Astra DB Pricing Model
paragraphs: A Free tier gives developers a recurring monthly usage credit, commonly cited as around 25 USD, covering up to roughly 80GB of storage and about 20 million read and write operations a month, enough for prototypes and small production workloads.

Beyond the free tier, a Pay As You Go plan charges per operation and per gigabyte of storage or transfer with no upfront commitment, while an Enterprise or Standard tier adds private endpoints, customer-managed encryption keys, and multi-region deployment, typically negotiated directly with sales.

features.description: Built on and continuing to support Apache Cassandra, Apache Pulsar, and OpenSearch open-source projects.

pros: Built on the mature, battle-tested Apache Cassandra database
Combines operational data and vector search in one database
Serverless pricing removes cluster management overhead
Backed by IBM's enterprise sales, support, and watsonx integration since 2025
Strong multi-cloud and multi-region deployment options
cons: Ownership change to IBM creates roadmap and pricing uncertainty for some customers
Usage-based pricing can be harder to predict than flat-rate alternatives
Cassandra's data model has a learning curve compared to relational databases
Enterprise-tier features require a negotiated, non-public quote
Smaller open-source mindshare than some newer vector-native databases

faqs.question: Did IBM acquire DataStax?
faqs.answer: Yes, IBM announced its agreement to acquire DataStax on February 25, 2025, and the deal closed on May 28, 2025.

faqs.question: What is Astra DB?
faqs.answer: Astra DB is a fully managed, cloud-native NoSQL and vector database-as-a-service built on Apache Cassandra.

faqs.question: Is Astra DB built on Apache Cassandra?
faqs.answer: Yes, Astra DB is built on Apache Cassandra and is offered as a managed, serverless cloud service.

faqs.question: Does Astra DB support vector search for AI applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, Astra DB includes native vector search and storage for retrieval-augmented generation and other AI use cases.

faqs.question: What is Langflow?
faqs.answer: Langflow is an open-source, low-code visual tool for building applications powered by large language models, maintained by DataStax.

faqs.question: Is there a free tier for Astra DB?
faqs.answer: Yes, Astra DB offers a Free tier with a recurring monthly usage credit covering meaningful storage and operations.

faqs.question: Where was DataStax headquartered before the IBM acquisition?
faqs.answer: DataStax was headquartered in Santa Clara, California, before its technology became part of IBM's product portfolio.

enrichment.short_description: DataVisor is an AI-powered fraud and risk management platform used by banks, fintechs, and enterprises to detect fraud, account takeover, and money laundering in real time. It combines unsupervised machine learning, rules, decision automation, and AI agents to catch new attack patterns that purely rule-based or supervised systems tend to miss. DataVisor was founded in 2013 and is headquartered in Mountain View, California, with additional offices in China.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom enterprise subscription, priced by data volume, protected accounts, and deployed modules
enrichment.starting_price: Pricing available on request; no published flat starting price
enrichment.headquarters: Mountain View, California, United States (with offices in Beijing and Shanghai, China)
enrichment.seo_title: DataVisor Review 2026: AI Fraud Platform Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DataVisor is an AI-powered fraud and AML platform for banks and enterprises. See 2026 pricing model, key features, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: DataVisor is a fraud and risk management platform that uses artificial intelligence to help banks, fintech companies, payment providers, and other enterprises detect and stop fraud, account takeover, money laundering, and other financial crime in real time. The company was founded in 2013 and is headquartered in Mountain View, California, with additional offices in Beijing and Shanghai, China, and employs roughly 150 to 165 people. DataVisor built its early reputation on unsupervised machine learning, an approach that looks for coordinated, suspicious patterns across large numbers of accounts and events without requiring pre-labeled examples of fraud, which lets it catch new and previously unseen attack patterns rather than only the fraud types a supervised model has already been trained to recognize. The core DataVisor Fraud and Risk Platform bundles several detection approaches into one system. It can verify new customers during onboarding while blocking synthetic identities and stolen-identity applications, detect account takeover by correlating unusual logins, device changes, and behavioral signals in real time, and identify counterfeit payment instruments and card-testing attacks within milliseconds of a transaction. The platform is built to operate at high throughput and low latency, reportedly capable of processing more than 15,000 queries per second while computing rich behavioral and risk features in under 100 milliseconds, which is necessary for use cases like point-of-sale authorization or login screening where a decision has to be made before a transaction or session completes. DataVisor states that its combined AI approach can increase fraud detection coverage by up to roughly 92 percent compared to rules and supervised models alone, primarily by surfacing coordinated attack rings and brand-new fraud patterns that neither static rules nor models trained only on historical fraud can see. Beyond core detection, the platform includes a Feature Platform for real-time feature computation, a visual Rules Engine that lets fraud and risk analysts create and tune detection rules without engineering support, and a Decision Flow module that uses a drag-and-drop interface to build multi-step fraud decisioning strategies, such as routing a suspicious transaction through additional verification steps rather than an outright block. DataVisor has continued to expand its AI capabilities with conversational AI agents that can execute across fraud and anti-money-laundering workflows end to end, covering tasks from strategy design and rule tuning to case investigation and regulatory reporting, aiming to reduce the manual analyst workload involved in tuning a fraud program and writing up investigation findings. DataVisor does not publish flat, self-serve pricing. Instead, the company uses a custom, subscription-based enterprise pricing model, with cost typically driven by the volume of transactions or events analyzed, the number of user accounts or customers being protected, which specific fraud and anti-money-laundering modules are deployed, for example onboarding fraud, account takeover protection, payments fraud, or AML case management, and the overall scale of the deployment. Prospective customers work directly with DataVisor's sales team to receive a quote tailored to their transaction volume and required modules; some independent reviews describe the platform's pricing as being oriented toward mid-size and large enterprises rather than very small businesses or individual users, reflecting its focus on financial institutions, payment companies, and other organizations with meaningful fraud exposure and dedicated risk teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Unsupervised Machine Learning for Fraud Detection
paragraphs: DataVisor built its early reputation on unsupervised machine learning, an approach that looks for coordinated, suspicious patterns across large numbers of accounts and events without requiring pre-labeled fraud examples, which helps it catch new attack patterns rather than only fraud types a supervised model has already seen.

The platform is built for high throughput and low latency, reportedly capable of processing more than 15,000 queries per second while computing risk features in under 100 milliseconds, which matters for real-time decisions like transaction authorization or login screening.

long_form_blocks.heading: Platform Modules: Rules, Decisioning, and AI Agents
paragraphs: The platform includes a Feature Platform for real-time feature computation, a visual Rules Engine that lets analysts build and tune rules without engineering support, and a Decision Flow module for constructing multi-step fraud decisioning strategies through a drag-and-drop interface.

DataVisor has expanded into conversational AI agents that can execute across fraud and anti-money-laundering workflows end to end, covering strategy design, rule tuning, investigation, and reporting tasks that previously required manual analyst work.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Model and Best Fit
paragraphs: DataVisor uses custom, subscription-based enterprise pricing driven by data volume, the number of protected accounts, and which fraud and anti-money-laundering modules an organization deploys, with quotes provided directly by DataVisor's sales team.

The platform is oriented toward mid-size and large financial institutions, payment companies, and other enterprises with meaningful fraud exposure and dedicated risk teams, rather than very small businesses or individual users.

Full capabilities depend on which modules an organization purchases

faqs.question: What does DataVisor do?
faqs.answer: DataVisor is an AI-powered fraud and risk management platform that detects fraud, account takeover, and money laundering in real time for banks and enterprises.

faqs.question: Who uses DataVisor?
faqs.answer: DataVisor is used by financial institutions, fintech companies, payment providers, and other enterprises with meaningful fraud exposure.

faqs.question: How does DataVisor detect fraud without labeled data?
faqs.answer: DataVisor uses unsupervised machine learning to find coordinated, suspicious patterns across accounts and events without needing prior labeled fraud examples.

faqs.question: Does DataVisor handle anti-money-laundering as well as fraud?
faqs.answer: Yes, DataVisor's platform includes AML capabilities alongside fraud detection, including AI agents for AML case workflows.

faqs.question: How much does DataVisor cost?
faqs.answer: DataVisor uses custom enterprise pricing based on data volume, protected accounts, and deployed modules; pricing is available on request.

faqs.question: Where is DataVisor headquartered?
faqs.answer: DataVisor is headquartered in Mountain View, California, with additional offices in Beijing and Shanghai, China.

faqs.question: When was DataVisor founded?
faqs.answer: DataVisor was founded in 2013.

faqs.question: Does DataVisor use AI agents?
faqs.answer: Yes, DataVisor has introduced conversational AI agents that execute across fraud and anti-money-laundering workflows end to end.

use_cases.title: Account takeover prevention
use_cases.description: Detect suspicious logins and device changes to stop account takeover during customer login.

use_cases.title: Onboarding fraud screening
use_cases.description: Screen new customer applications for synthetic identity and stolen-identity fraud.

use_cases.description: Block card-testing and counterfeit payment attacks in real time at checkout or authorization.

use_cases.description: Use AI agents to automate parts of anti-money-laundering investigation and reporting workflows.

enrichment.short_description: datelist.io is an online appointment booking and scheduling system that lets service businesses such as salons, restaurants, consultants, and event organizers accept digital reservations around the clock from a website or social media page. It automates confirmation and reminder messages, accepts online payment, and connects to personal calendars, CRM, invoicing, and newsletter tools. The product is made by ACL Software SRL, based in Temploux, Belgium, with plans starting at 9 euros per month.
enrichment.company_size: Small, privately held company (exact headcount not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: datelist.io is an online appointment booking system for salons, restaurants, and consultants. See 2026 pricing, key features, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: datelist.io, generally referred to as Datelist, is an online appointment booking and scheduling platform built by ACL Software SRL, a company based in Temploux, Belgium. It lets service-based businesses, including salons, spas, restaurants, cleaning companies, consultants, medical and coaching practices, driving schools, photographers, and event venues, offer customers a fully digital way to reserve an appointment or table at any hour of the day, without a staff member needing to answer the phone or manually check a paper diary. Customers book directly from the business's own website or from links shared on social media, choosing an available appointment window from a calendar the business controls. The platform is built around automating the administrative overhead of appointment-based businesses. Once a booking is made, datelist.io can automatically send confirmation and reminder messages by SMS or email a set number of minutes or hours before the appointment, which is intended to reduce no-shows and last-minute cancellations, a persistent cost for service businesses that block out staff time for a reservation that never arrives. The system can also collect payment directly at the time of booking, supporting multiple payment methods including card payments and, for the Belgian and broader European market, Bancontact, which lets a business require a deposit or full prepayment before confirming an appointment. datelist.io synchronizes with the business owner's or staff member's personal calendar, so availability shown to customers reflects real commitments and does not create double-bookings, and notifications flow both ways as new appointments are added. The platform is also designed to connect to other tools a small business already uses, including customer relationship management systems, newsletter and email marketing tools, and invoicing software, so a booking can trigger downstream workflows like adding a client to a mailing list or generating an invoice. datelist.io is also built to be customized to match a business's branding: users can set their own colors and visual style directly from the web platform so the booking page feels like a natural extension of the business's existing website rather than a generic third-party form. The company publishes case-study style landing pages tailored to specific industries, including cleaning services, consulting, events, and restaurants, each describing how the general booking, reminder, and payment features apply to that industry's particular workflow, such as table turnover for restaurants or session-based billing for consultants. Pricing for datelist.io is unusually simple compared to many software-as-a-service scheduling tools: rather than a tiered ladder of plans gated by feature or user count, the company offers a single, all-inclusive subscription starting at 9 euros per month, which the company states covers support and account management with no hidden fees. This flat-rate approach is aimed at small and solo service businesses that want predictable costs and do not want to evaluate multiple pricing tiers to figure out which features they need. The platform's marketing and help center content is available in both English and French, reflecting its Belgian, French-speaking home market, and it integrates with workplace tools including Slack and the Pipedrive CRM through listed marketplace apps, extending its usefulness beyond a standalone booking widget into broader business notification and sales workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Booking, Reminders, and Payment
paragraphs: Customers book an available appointment directly from a business's website or social media links at any hour, and once a booking is made, datelist.io can automatically send confirmation and reminder messages by SMS or email to help reduce no-shows and last-minute cancellations.

The platform can also collect payment or a deposit at the time of booking, supporting multiple payment methods including card payments and Bancontact, and it synchronizes two-way with the business owner's personal calendar to avoid double-booking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Branding and Business Tool Integrations
paragraphs: Businesses can customize the booking page's colors and style directly from the web platform so it matches their existing website rather than looking like a generic third-party form.

datelist.io connects to CRM, invoicing, and newsletter tools, and is listed on the Slack and Pipedrive marketplaces, letting a booking trigger downstream workflows such as adding a client to a mailing list or generating an invoice.

long_form_blocks.heading: A Single, Flat-Rate Pricing Plan
paragraphs: Rather than offering multiple tiers gated by feature or user count, datelist.io is sold as one all-inclusive subscription starting at 9 EUR per month, which the company states covers support with no hidden fees.
This flat pricing approach is aimed at small and solo service businesses that want a predictable monthly cost without needing to compare which features are locked behind higher tiers.

features.title: 24/7 online appointment booking
features.description: Customers can book an appointment at any hour directly from a website or social media links.

features.title: Automated confirmation and reminders
features.description: Sends automatic SMS and email confirmations and reminders to help reduce no-shows.

features.title: Online payment collection
features.description: Collects payment or deposits at the time of booking through multiple payment methods.

features.title: Two-way calendar synchronization
features.description: Syncs with the business owner's personal calendar to keep availability accurate and prevent double-booking.

features.title: Custom branding
features.description: Lets businesses set their own colors and style so the booking page matches their website.

features.title: CRM and invoicing integrations
features.description: Connects to CRM, invoicing, and newsletter tools so a booking can trigger downstream workflows.

features.title: Industry-specific workflows
features.description: Provides tailored booking setups for industries like restaurants, salons, consulting, and events.

features.title: Slack and Pipedrive marketplace apps
features.description: Integrates with Slack and Pipedrive CRM through listed marketplace applications.

pros: Simple, flat, all-inclusive monthly price with no tiers to compare
Reduces no-shows through automated reminders
Accepts payment and deposits directly at booking time
Easy visual customization to match a business's branding
Covers a wide range of service-business industries out of the box
cons: Founding year and team size are not publicly disclosed
Primarily supports French and English, which may limit reach outside those markets
Single flat plan offers less flexibility than tiered competitors for very different business sizes
Smaller brand recognition than larger scheduling platforms
Deposit and payment options may vary by supported payment method and region

faqs.question: What is datelist.io used for?
faqs.answer: datelist.io is used by service businesses to let customers book appointments or reservations online at any time, with automated reminders and payment collection.

faqs.question: How much does datelist.io cost?
faqs.answer: datelist.io is sold as a single, all-inclusive plan starting at 9 EUR per month.

faqs.question: Does datelist.io send appointment reminders?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can automatically send SMS and email confirmation and reminder messages before each appointment.

faqs.question: Can customers pay online when booking with datelist.io?
faqs.answer: Yes, datelist.io supports collecting payment or deposits at the time of booking through multiple payment methods.

faqs.question: What industries does datelist.io support?
faqs.answer: datelist.io serves salons, restaurants, consultants, cleaning companies, event organizers, and other appointment-based businesses.

faqs.question: Does datelist.io integrate with other business tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, it connects to CRM, invoicing, and newsletter tools, and is available through the Slack and Pipedrive marketplaces.

faqs.question: Who makes datelist.io?
faqs.answer: datelist.io is made by ACL Software SRL, a company based in Temploux, Belgium.

faqs.question: Is datelist.io available in English?
faqs.answer: Yes, datelist.io's platform and help center content are available in both English and French.
